coredata.dc:title: Rational design of an “on-off-on” fluorescent switch for Cu2+ and histidine based on chiral macrocyclic dioxopolyamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiaohuan
Li, Kaiqun
Wang, Xu
Xia, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
A novel chemosensor 1 having a chiral macrocyclic dioxopolyamine of C 2 symmetry as a receptor and anthracene as a signal unit has been designed and synthesized for cations and α-amino acids recognition in DMSO-HEPES buffer (1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). The ligand exhibited selective response to Cu2+ even in the presence of other metal ions with a fluorescence “switch-off” behavior. Additionally, the in situ generated 1-Cu
2+ ensemble displayed specific recognition to histidine by a “switch-on” fluorescence response. For this dual functional switch, its sensing behavior via a displacement mode was confirmed by 1H NMR titration and ESI mass spectroscopy. Sequential “on-off-on” fluorescence responses of 1 to Cu2+ and histidine are successfully applied in HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Histidine recognition
Chiral macrocycle
Fluorescent sensor
Displacement mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding Studies of 1 to Metal Ions

The Recognition Behavior of 1-Cu
2+ Ensemble to Histidine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of 1 (50 μM) before and after addition of an equivalent amount of various metal ions (DMSO-HEPES buffer, 1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). (b) Fluorescence titration profile of 1 (50 μM) upon addition of copper ions from 0 to 2.0 equiv. Inset: plot of fluorescence intensity at 416 nm versus the equivalents of copper ions added.

(a) Relative fluorescence response of 1 (50 μM) in the selectivity and competitive experiments with various metal ions (1 equiv.) in DMSO-HEPES buffer (1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). (b) ESI-MS spectrum of 1 after interaction with equivalent amount of Cu2+.

(a) Fluorescence titration profile of 1-Cu
2+ (50 μM) upon addition of d-His in DMSO-HEPES buffer (1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). Inset: photographs taken under a UV lamp (λ = 365 nm) of the ligand 1 (5 μM), 1 + Cu2+ (5 μM), and 1 + Cu2+ + d-His (50 μM). (b) Plots of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of 1-Cu
2+ against l- and d-His added. I and I0 represent the fluorescence intensity of 1-Cu
2+ in the presence and absence of guests, respectively.

Relative fluorescence intensity change of 1-Cu
2+ against various α-amino acids (10 equiv.) added. I and I0 represent the fluorescence intensity of 1-Cu
2+ in the presence and absence of α-amino acids, respectively.
Partial 1H NMR spectra of probe 1 in DMSO‑d 6 with titration of Cu2+ and d-His.

Confocal fluorescence (a-c) in situ images of Hela cells. (a) was created after the addition of 1; (b) was created after the addition of Cu2+ to ligand 1; (c) was created after the addition of S2− to the in-situ generated 1-Cu
2+ complex. (d) Plot of relative intracellular fluorescence intensity of different procedures.

head.ce:title: Rational design of an “on-off-on” fluorescent switch for Cu2+ and histidine based on chiral macrocyclic dioxopolyamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel chemosensor 1 having a chiral macrocyclic dioxopolyamine of C 2 symmetry as a receptor and anthracene as a signal unit has been designed and synthesized for cations and α-amino acids recognition in DMSO-HEPES buffer (1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). The ligand exhibited selective response to Cu2+ even in the presence of other metal ions with a fluorescence “switch-off” behavior. Additionally, the in situ generated 1-Cu
2+ ensemble displayed specific recognition to histidine by a “switch-on” fluorescence response. For this dual functional switch, its sensing behavior via a displacement mode was confirmed by 1H NMR titration and ESI mass spectroscopy. Sequential “on-off-on” fluorescence responses of 1 to Cu2+ and histidine are successfully applied in HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, great attention has been drawn to explore the systems for specific detection of biologically relevant cations [1], anions [2] and neutral α-amino acids [3]. As the third most abundant transition metal ions after iron and zinc, the deficiency of CuII can affect enzyme activity, while its excess may cause the damage of the kidneys and liver [4]. Thus, the distribution of Cu2+ ion in life system has to be highly controlled, and different categories of sensors have been explored for CuII recognition with exceptional selectivity and sensitivity [1,4–7]. To further exploit the paramagnetic ability of Cu2+ induced fluorescence quenching, sensing ensemble systems have been developed for selective detection of cyanide [8], glutathione [9], histidine [10], ATP/ADP [11,12], cysteine [13] and sulfide [4] detection with a sequential “On-Off-On” response.

Being an essential α-amino acid in a biological system, histidine serves as a neurotransmitter in the central nervous system and regulates metal transmission process. The deficiency of histidine could impair the nutritional state with chronic kidney disease, whereas excessive histidine could affect a variety of functions in humans [14,15]. Thus, detection of histidine especially in biological samples has attracted considerable attention. Among the techniques generally employed for histidine detection, including colorimetric detection [16], electrochemical methods [17], and circular dichroism (CD) spectra [14], fluorescence-based sensing is of great advantage due to its high sensitivity, easy portability and in situ monitoring with rapid response [10,15,18–22]. In fact, a variety of fluorophore-crafted systems have been utilized for histidine detection, such as metal complexes [15,23], polymers [14], MOFs [20], carbon nanoparticle [24], etc. However, it is worth noting that the histidine detection is always interfered by cysteine [15,25,26] and tryptophan [18]. Therefore, the development of fluorescent probes for specific detection of histidine in aqueous solution is always in demand.

Macrocyclic compounds are an important category in the field of supramolecular chemistry [27]. By delicate design of the structures, the rigid macrocycles always have superspecific recognition abilities. At the same time, metal-macrocycle ensemble system is preferred by a significant improvement in the water solubility, which favored their biological application. Utilization of metal-chiral macrocycle complexes has been rarely explored for α-amino acids recognition. Herein, the rigid C 2 symmetric macrocycle was combined with anthracene moiety to develop a fluorescent ligand 1, which shows strong affinity for Cu2+ in aqueous solution. The in situ generated 1-Cu
2+ ensemble exhibited specific selectivity towards histidine, which may be ascribed to the unique property of the chiral macrocycle structure.

The perchlorate salts of metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Co2+ were purchased from Aldrich chemicals. Eight α-amino acids and their enantiomer, such as l/d-Alanine (Ala), l/d-Phenylalanine (Phe), l/d-Histidine (His), l/d-Arginine (Arg), l/d-Tryptophan (Trp), l/d-Aspartic acid (Asp), l/d-cysteine and l/d-cystine were detected directly without modification. Acetonitrile was purified prior to use and other reagents were purchased and used without further purifications. The macrocyclic dioxopolyamine was obtained following published procedures [28]. NMR spectra were recorded on a Varian Mercury 300 spectrometer operating at 300 MHz for 1H and 75 MHz for 13C relative to tetramethylsilane as internal standard. Absorption spectra were identified on UV-2501 PC spectrophotometer.

A mixture of 9-(chloromethyl) anthracene (0.25 g, 0.85 mmol), the macrocyclic dioxopolyamine (0.25 g, 0.77 mmol), potassium iodide (0.3 g, 1.81 mmol) and dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (0.3 g, 1.40 mmol) in 25 mL anhydrous acetonitrile was stirred and heated under reflux for 2 days. The solvent was removed under reduced pressure to obtain a solid residue, which was taken up in dichloromethane, washed with water and brine. The organic layer was dried and evaporated to furnish the crude product, purified using silica gel column chromatography with ethyl acetate/methanol (70:1) as eluting solvent (74.7% yield) to get a pure product 1. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 300 MHz): δ 8.57 (s, 1H), 8.41–8.44 (m, 2H), 8.06–8.10 (m, 2H), 7.51–7.52 (m, 4H), 7.41 (s, 2H) 4.69–4.73 (d, J = 12.8 Hz, 1H), 4.24–4.28 (d, J = 13.1 Hz, 1H), 2.99–3.02 (m, 2H), 2.78–2.91 (m, 3H), 2.71–2.78 (m, 6H), 2.32–2.38 (m, 2H), 2.02–2.05 (m, 2H), 1.68–1.91 (m, 2H), 1.53–1.56 (m, 2H), 1.19–1.23 (m, 5H); 13C NMR (CDCl3, 75 MHz): δ 174.35, 134.08, 131.38, 129.15, 128.70, 127.94, 127.19, 126.15, 124.80, 124.53, 68.42, 55.85, 53.91, 53.38, 50.85, 36.37, 23.78 ESI-MS m/z (%): 514.17 ((M + H)+, 100).

HeLa cells were cultured in a medium consisted of 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Sigma) and 80 μg/mL penicillin-streptomycin, which were grown at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. Confocal fluorescence images were obtained by a TCS SP5 laser scanning microscope equipped with a 63× oil immersion objective (Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding Studies of 1 to Metal Ions
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the recognition ability of receptor 1 towards various metal ions was investigated by the fluorescence titration in DMSO-HEPES buffer (1:9, v/v, pH 7.2). The results indicated that compared to other metal ions, Cu2+ induced the most significant fluorescence reduction as displayed in Fig. 1a. With an increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence of 1 was quenched gradually (Fig. 1b). Its intensity is reduced to around 30% after the equivalent amount of Cu2+ addition as listed in the insert of Fig. 1b, which is due to the paramagnetic fluorescence quenching ability of Cu2+ [19,29]. UV–Vis titration experiments of 1 with Cu2+ were conducted under the same conditions (Fig. S4). Even after 20 equiv. of Cu2+ addition, no significant change of the UV–vis absorption bands of the anthracene moiety was observed, indicating a classic PET process occurred [30].

In order to further confirm the selectivity of 1 towards Cu2+, competition experiments were carried out by addition of 1 equiv. of Cu2+ to the probe solutions in the presence of 10 equiv. of other common metal ions. It seemed that the coexisting ions did not show obvious interference on the Cu2+ ion detection (Fig. 2a). For the formed complex, a molecular ion peak m/z at 575.35 in mass spectrum was observed with a 100% relative abundance, indicating a 1: 1 stoichiometry between probe 1 and Cu2+. The association constant established following the Benesi–Hildebrand equation by fluorescence titration is 2.66 × 104 M−1 (Fig. S5). The detection limit of 7.4 μM was estimated by the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the intercept of the regression line and the y-axis, and k is the slope of the calibration curve between emission intensity vs Cu2+ concentrations (Fig. S6). It is less than the copper ions level (20 μM) of drinking water defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The Recognition Behavior of 1-Cu
2+ Ensemble to Histidine
ce:section.ce:para:
As the key constituents of proteins, α-amino acids play an essential role in the metabolic processes of living bodies. Histidine (His) is critical to the control of metal ions transmission in biological system. Herein, after coordinating probe 1 to Cu2+, the ensemble was applied for sequential recognition of α-amino acids. Selectivity of 1-Cu
2+ to a series of α-amino acids enantiomers, such as l/d-Ala, l/d-Phe, l/d-His, l/d-Trp, l/d-Arg, l/d-Asp, l/d-cysteine and l/d-cystine, was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence of 1-Cu
2+ recovered gradually upon the addition of d-His, and the photographs taken under a UV lamp (λ = 365 nm) of the ligand 1 (5 μM), 1 + Cu2+ (5 μM), and 1 + Cu2+ + d-His (50 μM) clearly indicate an “on-off-on” mechanism. For its enantiomer l-His, the induced fluorescence enhancement of 1-Cu
2+ ensemble is lower than that of d-His (Fig. 3b). Although several chemosensors have been designed for histidine recognition, their enantioselectivity has been rarely studied. Even optically pure crown ether-terpyridine chelated with Zn2+ has been reported to show little enantioselectivity between l- and d-His [21]. In our case, d-His induced a more significant fluorescence recovery, which may be ascribed to the steric effect induced by the rigid chiral macrocycle. To make a comparison, the fluorescence vibrations of 1-Cu
2+ to other guests were also studied under the same conditions. Actually, no significant fluorescence recovery was observed with other α-amino acids addition as shown in Fig. 4 . The results demonstrated that 1-Cu
2+ ensemble has specific recognition ability towards His in aqueous solution, and the detection limits for l-His and d-His were determined to be 19 and 8 μM, respectively. Moreover, Job's plot analysis was also carried out, which indicated a 1:2 binding model of Cu2+ and l-/d-His to form the Cu2+-His complex [19,22]. Titration experiments of 1-Cu
2+ with α-amino acids were further performed by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Even up to 20 equiv. of d-His addition, the UV–vis absorption bands did not exhibit a significant change (Fig. S7), indicating a PET process occurred [30].

In order to further study the interactions between 1 and guests, the 1H NMR titration experiments of 1 with Cu2+ and d-His were performed in DMSO‑d
6 . It is well known that the paramagnetic property of Cu2+ always affects the 1H NMR frequency of protons close to its binding sites [7,8,10]. When 0.5 equiv. and 1.0 equiv. of Cu(ClO4)2 were added gradually, the peaks of 1 broadened (Fig. 5b and c), indicating the formation of 1-Cu
2+ ensemble. Especially upon 1 equiv. of Cu2+ addition, the proton signals of CH2 adjacent to anthracene (An-CH 2) almost disappeared. At the same time, the signal peak of the amine proton (N-H) peak at 7.40 ppm broadened and half-reduced (Fig. 5c), consistent with our ESI-MS results that one of the amines deprotonated for 1-Cu
2+ ensemble formation. Subsequently, the addition of d-His into the ensemble caused the resumption of the fine structure as shown in Fig. 5f. Actually, upon two equiv. of d-His addition, the signal peak of 1-Cu
2+ complex at 575.35 was not available in the ESI mass spectrum, while the molecular ion peak (at 514.04) of compound 1 reappeared with 100% abundance (Fig. S8). Thus, the sensing behavior to d-His was a displacement process. Due to strong chelation property of d-His, it competitively binds with Cu2+ to form a more stable complex Cu2+-d-His, freeing 1 from its metal ensemble (1-Cu
2+ ) [31,32].

Fluorescence imaging tests in HeLa cells were carried out to investigate the applications of 1 in Cu2+ and histidine recognition in the biological systems. Firstly, the applicability of 1 in cell imaging was investigated. HeLa cells were first stained with ligand 1 (5 μM, DMSO:DMEM = 1:99) at 37 °C for 30 min, followed by wash with PBS buffer for three times to remove the extracellular extra 1. As shown in Fig. 6a, the confocal fluorescence image clearly indicated that ligand 1 was cell permeable. While after treatment with Cu2+ (5 μM) for 30 min, the intracellular fluorescence was quite weak (Fig. 6b), suggesting an “on-off” fluorescence response of ligand 1 to Cu2+ ions. Furthermore, 1-Cu
2+ stained cells were treated with d-His (50 μM) for 1 h, and a remarkable fluorescence recovery was observed (Fig. 6c). The relative fluorescence intensity of each treatment was listed in Fig. 6d. These results demonstrate that the developed chemosensor 1 with sequential “on-off-on” response to Cu2+ and histidine can be successfully applied in the biological system.

Based on the specific chelation property of the C 2 symmetric macrocycle to metal ions and the unique optical property of anthracene, a fluorescent probe was obtained with specific recognition towards Cu2+ and histidine with a sequential “on-off-on” fluorescence response in aqueous solution. d-His induced a more significant fluorescence recovery than that of l-His may be due to the steric effect induced by the rigid chiral macrocycle. By a combination of the 1H NMR, ESI mass and UV spectroscopic analyses, the fluorescence response of 1-Cu
2+ to histidine is ascribed to the coordination of histidine to the Cu2+ center, which frees 1 from its metal ensemble to restore Cu2+ chelation induced emission.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) [Grant Nos. 21501055 and 11811520120] and Science and Technology Foundation for Creative Research Group of HBDE (T201810).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.042.
